target.name: (-)-Roxburghine C
target.motivation: Heteroyohimbines display intriguing bioactivities, including antihypertensive effects and benzodiazepine receptor ligand activity. Roxburghine C is an octacyclic compound with a nitrogen atom in the E ring, sharing stereochemistry with ajmalicine and mayumbine. The synthesis aims to establish a unified strategy for accessing these alkaloids.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Synthesis of (±)-roxburghine D from a carboxylic acid derivative.

success_criteria.free_text: Divergent enantioselective total synthesis of (-)-ajmalicine, (+)-mayumbine, and (-)-roxburghine C using a common intermediate.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the octacyclic core of roxburghine C via Pictet-Spengler reaction
key_disconnections.reasoning: This reaction is a known method for forming indole alkaloid frameworks and was envisioned to create the octacyclic structure of roxburghine C from a ketoamide precursor.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Lactone ring opening to form an amide
key_disconnections.reasoning: This allows for the introduction of the second tryptamine unit required for roxburghine C.
This step forms the pentacyclic lactone intermediate, a common precursor to all three target alkaloids.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Formation of pentacyclic compound 9 from N-acetoacetyl tryptamine and (E)-5-hydroxypent-2-enal
key_disconnections.reasoning: This reaction is highly enantioselective and diastereoselective, providing a key chiral intermediate.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy centers on a common pentacyclic lactone intermediate (8) which can be divergently transformed into the three target alkaloids. The key steps include an enantioselective organocatalytic reaction to build the initial pentacyclic core, followed by selective functional group transformations and a diastereoselective intramolecular Pictet-Spengler reaction for roxburghine C.

thought.background: N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10) is a known precursor for indole alkaloid synthesis.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10) from commercially available materials.
thought.decision: To synthesize N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10), chose heating tryptamine (12) with 2,2,6-trimethyl-4H-1,3-dioxin-4-one (13) in p-xylene, expecting an amide formation.

outcome.observation: N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10) was obtained in 84% yield.

evidence.text_span: Heating a $p$ -xylene solution of tryptamine 12 and 2,2,6- trimethyl-4H-1,3-dioxin-4-one (13) at $1 3 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for $4 5 ~ \mathrm { \ m i n }$ afforded the N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10) in $8 4\%$ yield.

thought.decision: To synthesize (E)-5-hydroxypent-2-enal (11), chose cross-metathesis of but-3-en-1-ol (14) with crotonaldehyde (15) in the presence of a Grubbs II catalyst, expecting the desired aldehyde.

evidence.text_span: Cross-metathesis of but-3-en-1-ol (14) with crotonaldehyde (15) in the presence of a Grubbs II catalyst provided (E)-5- hydroxypent-2-enal (11) in $67 \%$ yield.

thought.gap: Need to form the pentacyclic compound (3S, 15R)-9 with high diastereo- and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To form the pentacyclic compound (3S, 15R)-9, chose Franzen's procedure using Hayashi-Jørgensen catalyst (16) followed by AcCl treatment, expecting a highly selective organocatalytic reaction.

outcome.observation: Pentacyclic compound (3S, 15R)-9 was obtained with high ee and dr. It was then converted to N-Cbz derivative 17 without purification.

evidence.text_span: Following Franzen’ś procedure, stirring a dichloromethane solution of 10 and 11 with a catalytic amount of Hayashi−Jørgensen catalyst 16 (20 $\mod \%$ at $- 2 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ followed by addition of AcCl (10 equiv, rt) afforded the pentacyclic compound (3S, 15R)-9 ( $9 7\%$ ee, dr ${ 	t > } 2 0 { : } 1 $ ) which, without purification, was converted to N-Cbz derivative 17 in $7 2\%$ isolated yield.

thought.background: The pentacyclic compound 9 was converted to N-Cbz derivative 17.
thought.gap: Need to selectively reduce the amide in 17 to a tertiary amine.
thought.decision: To selectively reduce the amide 17 to tertiary amine 18, chose treatment with Meerwein's salt followed by NaBH4 reduction of the imino ether, expecting a selective reduction.

outcome.observation: Tertiary amine 18 was formed. This step is part of a sequence that yields 76% over 3 steps from 17.

evidence.text_span: Selective reduction of the amide 17 to tertiary amine 18 was realized by treatment of 17 with Meerwein’s salt followed by reduction of the resulting tetrafluoroborate salt of the imino ether with ${ \mathrm { N a B H } } _ { 4 }$ 18

thought.gap: Need to hydrate the enol ether function and reduce the resulting hydroxy ketone to a diol.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose treatment of crude 18 with 2 N HCl in THF, expecting diastereoselective hydration, followed by reduction of the crude hydroxy ketone with NaBH4, expecting diol formation.

outcome.observation: 1,5-diol 19 was obtained as a 3:1 mixture of two diastereoisomers (19a/19b) in 76% yield over 3 steps from 17. The major isomer 19a's stereochemistry was confirmed by X-ray crystallography.

evidence.text_span: Treating the crude tertiary amine 18 with 2 N HCl in THF at room temperature afforded a single (3S, 15R, 20R)- diastereomer resulting from the highly diastereoselective hydration of the enol ether function. Reduction of the crude hydroxy ketone by ${ \mathrm { N a B H } } _ { 4 }$ afforded 1,5-diol 19 as a 3:1 mixture of two diastereoisomers in $7 6\%$ yield from 17. The absolute and relative stereochemistry of major isomer 19a was determined by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

thought.gap: Need to selectively oxidize 1,5-diol 19 to the δ-lactone 8.
thought.decision: To achieve selective oxidation, various oxidation conditions were evaluated, including PCC, Fetizon's reagent, Swern oxidation, and Cu-nitroxyl-air.

outcome.observation: Selective oxidation of 1,5-diol to δ-lactone proved challenging with these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Selective oxidation of 1,5-diol to $\delta$ -lactone proved challenging. After evaluating various oxidation conditions (PCC, Fetizon’ś reagent, Swern oxidation, Cu-nitroxyl-air,19 etc.), the desired lactone 8 was isolated using either $\mathrm { { R u C l } } _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { { P P h } } _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 }$ $5 2\%$ yield)20 or Ley oxidation [TPAP (cat.), NMO, molecular sieves, $7 0\%$ yield]

thought.background: Previous attempts at selective oxidation of 1,5-diol 19 to δ-lactone 8 were unsuccessful.
thought.gap: Need an effective method for selective oxidation.
thought.decision: To achieve selective oxidation, chose Ley oxidation (TPAP, NMO, molecular sieves), expecting a better yield and selectivity.

outcome.observation: The desired lactone 8 was isolated in 70% yield (57% over 4 steps from 17) as two separable diastereoisomers (dr 3:1) using Ley oxidation. The keto-aldehyde byproduct was minimal (<10%). This sequence was performed on gram-scale without intermediate purification.

evidence.text_span: the desired lactone 8 was isolated using either $\mathrm { { R u C l } } _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { { P P h } } _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 }$ $5 2\%$ yield)20 or Ley oxidation [TPAP (cat.), NMO, molecular sieves, $7 0\%$ yield],21 with the keto-aldehyde as the only byproduct $\left( < 1 0\% ight)$ . Using Ley oxidation, the full sequence from 17 was performed on gram-scale yielding the desired lactone 8 as two separable diastereoisomers (dr 3:1) in $5 7\%$ yield, without requiring column chromatographic purification of the intermediates 18 and 19.

thought.gap: Need to convert lactone 8a to N-unprotected pentacyclic lactone 20.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose deprotonation with LHMDS followed by enolate trapping with dimethyl carbonate, and then treatment with sodium methoxide, expecting formation of the β-ketoester and subsequent deprotection.

outcome.observation: N-unprotected pentacyclic lactone 20 was obtained in 75% yield as a single diastereomer.

evidence.text_span: Deprotonation of 8a with LHMDS followed by enolate trapping with dimethyl carbonate yielded the $ eta$ -ketoester, which was subsequently treated with sodium methoxide to afford N-unprotected pentacyclic lactone 20 in $7 5\%$ yield (Scheme 3a). Although inconsequential, 20 was obtained as a single diastereomer, likely formed under thermodynamic control.

thought.decision: To achieve this, chose selective reduction of lactone 20 to lactol followed by dehydration, expecting formation of (-)-ajmalicine.

outcome.observation: (-)-Ajmalicine (1) was obtained in 57% yield over 2 steps. Its absolute and relative stereochemistry was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

evidence.text_span: Selective reduction of lactone 20 to lactol followed by dehydration yielded $( - )$ -ajmalicine 1 $5 7\%$ yield).5p Its absolute and relative stereochemistry was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

thought.decision: To achieve this, chose the same synthetic route as for (-)-ajmalicine, expecting formation of (+)-mayumbine.

reaction.time: 1 h then 2 h then 30 min then 1 h then 8 h

evidence.text_span: Following the same synthetic route, compound 8b was transformed to $\left( + ight)$ -mayumbine 2 in $4 0\%$ overall yield (Scheme 3b).

thought.gap: Need to introduce the second tryptamine unit for roxburghine C synthesis.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose AlMe3-mediated aminolysis of lactone 8 with N1-Boc tryptamine (21), expecting formation of amide 22.

evidence.text_span: The $\mathrm { AlM } {  f e } _ { 3 }$ -mediated aminolysis of lactone 8 with $N _ { I }$ -Boc tryptamine 21 (DCM, rt, 16 h) yielded 22 in $7 3\%$ 1 yield.

thought.gap: Need to oxidize the secondary alcohol in 22 to a ketone and then perform N-Boc deprotection and intramolecular Pictet-Spengler reaction to form the octacyclic compound 24.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose oxidation of 22 with IBX, followed by a one-pot N-Boc deprotection/intramolecular Pictet-Spengler reaction using trifluoroacetic acid, expecting formation of 24 with high diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Octacyclic compound 24 was obtained in 68% yield over 2 steps with excellent diastereoselectivity. The intermediate δ-ketoamide 23 was used without purification.

evidence.text_span: Oxidation of the secondary alcohol 22 cleanly afforded the $\delta$ -ketoamide 23 which was used, without purification, in the subsequent one-pot N-Boc deprotection/intramolecular Pictet−Spengler reaction. Gratifyingly, stirring a dichloro- methane solution of crude 23 in the presence of trifluoroacetic acid (40.0 equiv) afforded the octacyclic compound 24 in $6 8\%$ yield with excellent diastereoselectivity $\mathrm { ( d r > } 2 0 { : } 1 \mathrm { ) }$ ).

thought.gap: Need to introduce a methoxycarbonyl group and remove the N-Cbz group to form β-ketoester 25.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose methoxycarbonylation of 24 using LiTMP and dimethyl carbonate, followed by removal of the N-Cbz group with NaOMe, expecting formation of 25 as a single diastereoisomer.

reaction.time: 1 h then 1 h then 2 h then 30 min

outcome.observation: β-ketoester 25 was obtained in 75% yield as a single diastereoisomer. The absolute configuration at C19 was determined to be (S) by X-ray crystallography, matching (-)-roxburghine C.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent methoxycarbonylation of 24 (LTMP, dimethyl carbonate) followed by removal of the N-Cbz group (NaOMe) afforded the $ eta$ -ketoester 25 in $7 5\%$ yield as a single diastereoisomer. The absolute configuration of the newly formed stereocenter at C19 was determined to be (S) by X-ray crystallographic analysis of 25, confirming the (3S, 15S, 20R, 19S) stereochemistry, which matches that of $( - )$ -roxburghine C 3.

thought.gap: Need to convert the β-amido ester to the corresponding β-enamino ester to obtain (-)-roxburghine C (3).
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose reduction of 25 using Vaska's catalyst and tetramethyldisiloxane (TMDS) in dichloromethane, following Ye-Huang's procedure, expecting formation of (-)-roxburghine C.

outcome.observation: (-)-Roxburghine C (3) was obtained in 91% isolated yield. Approximately 200 mg of the natural product was prepared in a single batch from lactone 8 in 34% yield, and 14% overall yield from N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10).

evidence.text_span: Conversion of $ eta$ -amido ester to the corresponding $ eta$ -enamino ester was realized following Ye-Huang’s procedure.23 Specifically, reduction of 25 using Vaska’s catalyst24 and tetramethyldisiloxane (TMDS) in dichloromethane afforded $( - )$ -roxburghine C 3 in $9 1\%$ isolated yield. Approximately 200 mg of this natural product was prepared in a single batch from lactone 8 in $3 4\%$ yield and $1 4\%$ overall yield from the starting material, N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10).

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield from N-acetoacetyl tryptamine (10) to (-)-roxburghine C (3).

validation.significance: This work presents a divergent enantioselective total synthesis of three complex indole alkaloids: (-)-ajmalicine, (+)-mayumbine, and (-)-roxburghine C, using a common pentacyclic lactone intermediate. Key achievements include a highly selective organocatalytic reaction, a diastereoselective intramolecular Pictet-Spengler reaction, and a chemoselective reduction of a β-amidoester to a β-enaminoester. The synthesis provides access to these natural products in a short and enantioselective manner.

source.title: Divergent Enantioselective Total Synthesis of (-)-Ajmalicine, (+)-Mayumbine, and (-)-Roxburghine C.
source.authors: Goëlo V, Wang Q, Zhu J.
